meta.title: Physical Therapy Practice Software | RTM Billing & Multi-Patient Flow | clinIQ
meta.description: Complete guide to PT practice operations. $10,400/month in musculoskeletal RTM. Multi-patient scheduling visibility. Authorization tracking. Visit cap management.

hero.subhead: Your PT patients already do home exercises between visits — that's billable RTM. Your therapists already juggle multiple patients — that needs visibility. Your authorizations already expire — that needs tracking. clinIQ captures the revenue and fixes the operations.

sections.content:
Physical therapy practices face a unique operational challenge: therapists treat multiple patients simultaneously, but most software treats PT like a one-patient-at-a-time specialty.

The typical PT workflow: Therapist has 3-4 patients in a given hour. One is doing exercises independently. One is on modalities. One needs hands-on treatment. One just arrived and needs setup. The therapist rotates between them.

The problem: Nobody can see which patient needs the therapist right now. Patients sit waiting on a mat wondering if they've been forgotten. The therapist doesn't know which patient has been waiting longest. The front desk doesn't know if patients are finishing on time.

Beyond patient flow, PT practices leave money on the table:

Musculoskeletal RTM: Your patients do home exercise programs between visits. They should be logging compliance, pain levels, and functional progress. That's Remote Therapeutic Monitoring — billable at $100-130/patient/month. Most PT practices bill zero RTM.

Authorization management: Payers authorize 12-20 visits. Patient uses 18, needs more, authorization expired. Nobody tracked it. Revenue lost, patient undertreated.

Therapist productivity: Without visibility, therapists can't optimize their patient load. They run behind without knowing it. Patients wait without anyone noticing.

This guide covers the systems that capture revenue and fix flow.

Remote Therapeutic Monitoring for musculoskeletal conditions launched in 2022. CPT 98975, 98977, 98980, 98981. This is specifically designed for conditions like the ones PT treats.

Your patients are already doing home exercises. They're supposed to track compliance. They're supposed to report pain levels. They're supposed to note functional improvements. Most don't track systematically — they show up to appointments and report verbally.

RTM systematizes that tracking and makes it billable.

**What qualifies for musculoskeletal RTM:**

Home exercise program compliance: Did the patient do their exercises? How many times? Which ones were difficult?

Pain tracking: Pain levels during activity, at rest, overnight. Trends over time.

Functional status: Can they climb stairs? Lift overhead? Walk without pain? Return to sport activities?

Activity progression: Are they advancing per protocol? Stuck at a plateau? Regressing?

**The CPT codes:**

98975: RTM device/software setup — one-time enrollment, patient education. ~$20-25.
98977: RTM musculoskeletal device supply — monthly. ~$50-60.
98980: RTM treatment management — first 20 minutes of clinical review per month. ~$50-70.
98981: Additional 20-minute increments.

Typical monthly billing: 98977 + 98980 = $100-130/patient.

**The math for a PT practice:**

80 appropriate patients × $130/month = $10,400/month = $124,800/year.

That's revenue for monitoring you're already supposed to be doing — tracking home program compliance and adjusting treatment based on between-visit progress.

**What clinIQ does for PT RTM:**

Patient app for HEP tracking: Exercises assigned, compliance logged, pain reported. Patient checks off completed exercises. Logs pain before/after.

Therapist dashboard: Which patients are compliant? Which aren't exercising? Which are reporting increased pain? Informs treatment decisions.

Automatic time tracking: Data review documented toward billing threshold. No manual time logs.

Outcome correlation: RTM data tied to functional outcomes. You can see whether home compliance predicts better results.

**The compliance advantage:**

Patients who track home exercises are more compliant. They know you're watching. RTM isn't just billing — it's better outcomes through accountability.

PT isn't one-patient-at-a-time medicine. A therapist might have:

Patient A: Doing independent exercises, needs check-in in 5 minutes
Patient B: On electrical stimulation, finishing in 8 minutes
Patient C: Needs hands-on manual therapy now
Patient D: Just arrived, needs warmup setup

The therapist rotates. But without visibility, no one knows who needs attention when.

**The visibility problem:**

Patient has been on the mat for 12 minutes. Is that right? Or did the therapist forget them?

Patient finished e-stim 5 minutes ago. Still lying there. Who knows?

Therapist is with Patient C. Patients A, B, and D are all waiting. Which one is most urgent?

**What clinIQ does for multi-patient flow:**

Patient status board: Every patient visible with current activity and time in activity.

Timer tracking: Patient started exercises at 10:15. It's now 10:27. Visual alert that they may be ready.

Therapist queue: Therapist sees their patients ranked by who needs attention soonest.

Finished alerts: Patient finished modality, timer goes off, visual indicator shows 'ready for therapist.'

Waiting patient alerts: Patient has been waiting for therapist attention for 8+ minutes. Escalation alert.

**The patient experience:**

Old experience: Patient finishes exercises, sits on mat wondering if therapist remembers them. Waits 10 minutes. Gets frustrated.

New experience: Patient finishes exercises. Within 2 minutes, therapist arrives. 'Great job, let's move to the next part of your session.'

The difference is visibility, not effort. Therapists want to provide attentive care — they just can't see who needs them when.

PT authorizations are a constant battle. Payers authorize a limited number of visits. Patients use them faster than expected. Authorization expires before treatment is complete. Nobody tracked it until the visit that gets denied.

**The authorization problem:**

Patient authorized for 12 visits. Using 2/week. By visit 10, you need to request more. But if nobody's tracking, visit 13 gets denied. Patient is undertreated or pays out of pocket.

Worst case: Patient hits visit cap, doesn't know it, shows up for appointment. Front desk discovers at check-in. Chaos.

**What clinIQ does for authorization tracking:**

Visits remaining: Every patient's authorization status visible — visits authorized, visits used, visits remaining.

Expiration tracking: Authorization expires on [date]. If patient isn't done, you know before it expires.

Reauthorization alerts: 'Patient has 3 visits remaining, treatment plan calls for 8 more. Request additional visits now.'

Cap visibility at scheduling: When scheduling, you see whether patient has visits remaining. Don't schedule visit 13 of a 12-visit auth.

**Annual benefit tracking:**

Many payers have annual PT visit limits (e.g., 30 visits/year combined for PT/OT/ST). Patients who've been to another therapist earlier in the year have fewer visits available.

clinIQ tracks both authorization-level limits and annual benefit limits where that information is available.

**The revenue protection:**

One denied visit = $100-150 lost revenue.

One patient who hits cap without reauthorization = multiple denied visits.

Authorization visibility protects revenue that would otherwise be lost to denial.

Therapist productivity directly determines PT practice revenue. A therapist treating 10 patients/day generates less than one treating 14. The difference is significant.

**What affects therapist productivity:**

Patient transitions: Time between patients. Therapist finishes with one patient, how quickly does the next one start?

Multi-patient management: Ability to effectively rotate between patients without wasted time.

Documentation burden: Time spent charting vs treating.

Schedule optimization: Right patient mix, right timing, minimal gaps.

**What clinIQ does for productivity:**

Patient ready visibility: Therapist knows when their next patient is ready. No walking to the front to ask.

Rotation efficiency: Visual queue showing which current patient needs attention soonest.

Time tracking: How long is each patient actually taking? Data for schedule optimization.

Productivity metrics: Patients per day, average visit duration, gap analysis.

**The benchmark question:**

How many patients can a PT treat per day? Varies by setting:

Outpatient orthopedic: 10-14 patients/day typical, 16+ in high-efficiency practices
Sports PT: Similar range, sometimes higher with athletes
Neurological PT: Lower volume due to complexity
Pediatric PT: Varies widely

clinIQ helps identify where your practice falls and what's limiting higher productivity.

Outcomes matter for payers, for patients, and for practice marketing. But tracking outcomes manually is tedious, and most practices don't do it systematically.

**What outcomes tracking provides:**

Payer documentation: 'Patient improved from 60% to 85% on LEFS' supports medical necessity for continued treatment.

Patient motivation: Showing patients their objective improvement keeps them engaged.

Practice marketing: '87% of patients achieve treatment goals' is a competitive differentiator.

Quality improvement: Which conditions do you treat best? Where do outcomes lag?

**Standard outcome measures for PT:**

Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) — low back
Neck Disability Index (NDI) — cervical
Lower Extremity Functional Scale (LEFS) — lower extremity
Upper Extremity Functional Scale (UEFS) — upper extremity
Disabilities of the Arm, Shoulder and Hand (DASH) — upper extremity
Numeric Pain Rating Scale (NPRS) — pain

**What clinIQ does for outcomes:**

Automated administration: Outcome measures sent to patients electronically at appropriate intervals (intake, mid-treatment, discharge).

Score calculation: Automatic scoring. Therapist sees results, not raw data.

Progress visualization: Patient improvement displayed graphically. Useful for patient motivation and documentation.

Aggregate analytics: Practice-wide outcomes by condition, by therapist. Identifies strengths and improvement opportunities.

RTM integration: Outcomes data feeds into RTM documentation. The same tracking supports both clinical decisions and billing.

PT practices have specific workflows that differ from other medical specialties. Implementation accounts for multi-patient treatment, authorization requirements, and outcome tracking.

**Week 1: Configuration**

We configure for your workflow: treatment areas, modality stations, therapist assignments, authorization tracking requirements, outcome measures used.

RTM templates configured for your common diagnoses: low back, cervical, shoulder, knee, etc.

**Week 2: Training and Go-Live**

Therapist training on patient flow visibility and RTM review. Front desk training on authorization tracking. Tech/aide training on patient status updates.

Go-live with clinIQ team available.

**Week 3 and Beyond**

RTM enrollment beginning. Patient flow visibility active. Authorization tracking running.

**ROI Timeline:**

Month 1: Patient flow improvements immediate. RTM enrollment starting.

Month 3: RTM billing at scale (50-80 patients). Authorization denials decreasing. Productivity improvements measurable.

Month 6: Full RTM revenue ($8,000-12,000/month). Authorization visibility preventing lost revenue. Outcomes data accumulating.

**ROI Math:**

RTM revenue: 80 patients × $120/month = $9,600/month = $115,200/year

Denial prevention: 5 prevented denials/month × $120/visit = $600/month = $7,200/year

Productivity improvement: 1 additional patient/therapist/day × 3 therapists × $100 = $300/day = $78,000/year

Conservative combined estimate: $150,000-200,000/year additional revenue.

**Investment:**

Professional: $499/month — Full suite including RTM, patient flow, authorization tracking, outcomes

Implementation: $750 one-time — Workflow mapping, configuration, training

Multi-site: Volume pricing for PT groups

faqs.q: Is musculoskeletal RTM billable for PT?
faqs.a: Yes. CPT 98975, 98977, 98980, 98981 specifically cover musculoskeletal RTM. Home exercise compliance, pain tracking, and functional status monitoring all qualify. Medicare and most commercial payers cover these codes.

faqs.q: How does multi-patient flow tracking work?
faqs.a: Every patient visible with current activity and time. Timers track how long patients have been at each station. Alerts when patients finish modalities or have been waiting for therapist attention. Therapist sees prioritized queue of who needs them.

faqs.q: Can we track authorization visit counts?
faqs.a: Yes. Visits authorized, visits used, visits remaining — visible per patient. Alerts when running low. Expiration dates tracked. Prevents scheduling visits that will be denied.

faqs.q: What about annual PT benefit limits?
faqs.a: We track both authorization-specific limits and annual benefit caps where that information is available from payer eligibility data.

faqs.q: Does this work with PTAs and techs?
faqs.a: Yes. Patient status can be updated by any staff member. Therapist supervision ratios respected. Different visibility for different roles.

faqs.q: How do patients track home exercises for RTM?
faqs.a: Patient app where exercises are assigned, compliance is logged, and pain is reported. Can integrate with existing HEP software or use clinIQ's exercise library.
ODI, NDI, LEFS, DASH, NPRS, and other standard PT outcome measures. Administered electronically at configurable intervals. Automatic scoring.
Two weeks typical. PT workflows are relatively standardized, making implementation straightforward.

cta.headline: See RTM revenue and patient flow visibility

testimonial.quote: Our therapists were running blind with 4 patients each. Now everyone can see who needs attention. Patient satisfaction went up, therapist stress went down, and we're billing $9K/month in RTM.
